explanation:
**Nutrition and fitness** are grounded in physics (energy balance) and biology (macronutrients, micronutrients). This calculator uses formulas validated by the scientific community (ACSM, ISSN, NIH).

## How It's Calculated

Mediterranean plan 1500–2500 kcal.

This calculator applies the formula with your inputs, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside reasonable bounds.

## Standard Calorie Distribution

| Macronutrient | % Calories | Grams/kcal |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 15–35% | 4 kcal/g |
| Carbohydrates | 45–65% | 4 kcal/g |
| Fats | 20–35% | 9 kcal/g |
| Alcohol | — | 7 kcal/g |
| Fiber | Minimum 25–38g/day | — |

## When to Consult a Nutritionist

- If you have medical conditions (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease) requiring personalized diet.
- If you want to gain significant muscle or compete in sports.
- If you have disordered eating patterns (binge eating, extreme restriction).
- If you've been working toward your goal for over 3 months without progress.
- To plan complete vegetarian or vegan nutrition.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.a: The Mediterranean diet emphasizes olive oil, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, fish, and moderate wine. Based on eating patterns of countries around the Mediterranean Sea, it's backed by decades of health research showing benefits for heart health and longevity.

faq.q: How many calories per day do I need?
faq.a: Daily calorie needs vary by age, sex, weight, and activity level. Generally: adult women 1800–2200 kcal, men 2200–3000 kcal. Use our TDEE calculator for a personalized estimate based on your specifics.
Standard distribution: Protein 15–35%, Carbohydrates 45–65%, Fats 20–35%. Aim for at least 25–38g fiber daily. These ratios provide balanced nutrition while supporting your health goals.

faq.q: How much protein should I eat daily?
faq.a: Sedentary: 0.8g per kg body weight. Active: 1.2–1.5g/kg. Building muscle: 1.6–2.2g/kg lean mass. Useful ceiling: ~2.2g/kg body weight.

faq.q: Is the Mediterranean diet backed by science?
faq.a: Yes. Multiple peer-reviewed studies show Mediterranean patterns reduce heart disease risk, improve brain health, and support healthy aging. Guidelines are current as of 2026.

faq.q: Can I lose weight on this diet?
faq.a: Yes. Weight loss requires a calorie deficit. The Mediterranean diet's focus on whole foods, healthy fats, and fiber makes it easier to maintain a deficit while staying satisfied.

faq.q: How fast will I see results?
faq.a: Healthy weight loss: 0.5–1 kg per week. Faster rates often mean muscle loss and rebound weight gain. Sustainable progress is gradual and consistent—that's what lasts long-term.

faq.q: Should I see a doctor before starting?
faq.a: Always consult your doctor before major dietary changes, especially with medical conditions (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease) or medications. A registered dietitian can provide personalized guidance.

faq.q: Do I need supplements on this plan?
faq.a: Not necessarily. A well-planned Mediterranean diet covers most nutrient needs. Consider B12 if vegetarian, vitamin D in northern climates, or omega-3s if fish intake is low. Consult a professional before supplementing.

faq.q: How do I customize this plan for my needs?
faq.a: Start with your calorie target and macronutrient ranges. Adjust portions based on hunger, energy, and results. Track intake for 2–4 weeks to see what works best for you.
